specialism.name: base-show
specialism.task:
Add or improve Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::T) and Base.summary(io::IO, x::T) methods for Julia types whose REPL representation is unhelpful or violates the rubric below. Default Julia show dumps every field recursively; types that hold DataFrames, large dictionaries, nested arrays, or many scalar fields produce screens of text at the REPL. Existing custom show methods may also violate the rubric (too long, allocation-unsafe, missing Base.summary delegation, or printing collection contents) and should be improved.
Base.summary provides the single-line header string Julia uses in error messages, array display prefixes, and as input to higher-level show routines. Defining it separately from Base.show lets other display contexts (e.g. showing a vector of your type) benefit from the compact description without duplicating logic.
This specialism produces compact, informative show and summary methods and accompanying unit tests so that interactive use of the package is pleasant without losing key summary information.

workflow.step: Enumerate concrete types defined in the package: grep -nrE '^(mutable )?struct ' src/ Exclude abstract types (abstract type ...) — they cannot be instantiated.

For each concrete type, assign it to exactly one category:
Category A — MISSING: no Base.show(io, ::MIME"text/plain", x::T) exists AND the type is "noisy" (holds a DataFrame, a Dict with many entries, a large Array, another noisy struct, or more than ~6 fields). These need new show and summary methods.
Category B — NEEDS IMPROVEMENT: a Base.show method exists but violates at least one rubric criterion: - output exceeds 10 lines for a typical instance - prints collection contents rather than counts/sizes - allocates inside show (collect, sort, filter, keys()/values() with iteration) - first line does not match what Base.summary would return (or Base.summary is absent) Read the existing method body before judging. These need targeted edits, not wholesale rewrites — preserve correct logic; fix only the failing criteria.
Category C — OK: a Base.show method exists and passes all rubric criteria. Skip.
Use these greps to locate existing methods: grep -nrE 'Base\.show' src/ grep -nrE 'Base\.summary' src/

Act on each category separately.
=== Category A (MISSING): write both Base.summary and Base.show from scratch. ===
3a. Base.summary — write first; Base.show will delegate to it.
function Base.summary(io::IO, x::T)
Must write exactly one fragment (no trailing newline) containing: - The bare type name (no type parameters). For parametric types such as `EnsembleKalmanProcess{FT,IT,P,...}`, write only `EnsembleKalmanProcess`. - Optionally, the most critical size hint in parentheses, e.g. "(N=50, iter=3)". Include only hints that are O(1) to compute. - No newline — Base.summary is a header fragment. Use `print`, not `println`.
3b. Base.show — delegates its first line to Base.summary.
function Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::T)
Must print: - `Base.summary(io, x)` then `println(io)` as the very first two statements. - 1–5 concise detail lines: key scalar fields (e.g. " process = Inversion()"), counts (e.g. " N_ensemble = 50"), iteration number, sizes of stored collections. Never print collection contents. - Total output: at most 10 lines.
Placement: adjacent to the type definition in the same source file (the existing convention — see `src/ParameterDistributions.jl:124–440`). Place Base.summary immediately before Base.show. Do not create a separate `src/show.jl` unless there is already one in the package.
=== Category B (NEEDS IMPROVEMENT): edit the existing method(s) minimally. ===
Read the existing method body in full before editing. Fix only the criteria that fail:
- Too many lines: remove or consolidate detail lines until output ≤ 10 lines for a typical instance. - Prints collection contents: replace with a count/size expression. - Allocation-unsafe: replace collect/sort/filter/keys(d) with O(1) alternatives (length, size, isempty, first on a lazy iterator). - Missing Base.summary or first line mismatch: add a Base.summary method and make the first two statements of Base.show `Base.summary(io, x); println(io)`. If a Base.summary already exists but the show method does not call it, patch show to call it instead of repeating the header logic.
Do not restructure passing logic. Preserve field order, labels, and intent where the existing output already satisfies the rubric.

Write or update unit tests for every method touched in step 3.
For Category A (new methods): add a new `@testset "show"` block. For Category B (improved methods): if a show test already exists, update its assertions to match the new output; if none exists, add one now.
Every test block must assert structural properties only — not exact strings — so that cosmetic edits do not break tests: - `occursin("BareTypeName", out)` — bare type name appears in show output. - `count(==('\n'), out) <= 10` — output is at most 10 lines. - `!isempty(sprint(Base.summary, instance))` — summary is non-empty. - `occursin("BareTypeName", sprint(Base.summary, instance))` — summary starts with type.
Capture output with: sprint(show, MIME("text/plain"), instance) # for Base.show sprint(summary, instance) # for Base.summary
Placement: `@testset "show"` block inside the matching per-module test file at `test/<Module>/runtests.jl`. Do not create a standalone `test/show/` directory.
Verify: load the package (julia --project -e 'using <PackageName>') and run the test suite. Confirm the new tests pass and no pre-existing tests regress.

rubric.criterion: Every Category A type has both a Base.show and a Base.summary method after the task. Every Category B type has all failing criteria resolved after the task.
Category B edits are surgical: only the lines that violate the rubric are changed. Correct logic, field order, and labels in existing methods are preserved unchanged.
show output is at most 10 lines for any instance, including edge cases (empty collections, zero-element structs, etc.).
The show method does not throw on any valid instance, including default-constructed or partially-populated instances.

Every data access inside a show method must be an O(1) metadata query: length(), size(), isempty(), first() on a lazy iterator, or direct field access. Never call collect(), sort(), filter(), keys()/values() followed by iteration, or any function that materialises a new collection from an existing one. This guarantees show is safe to call on structs holding millions of elements without triggering large allocations or hangs.
Unit tests assert structural properties (contains type name, line count), not exact strings. A cosmetic change to the show output does not break any test.
Pre-existing tests continue to pass; no unintended changes to other source files.

formatting.rule: Base.summary signature: Base.summary(io::IO, x::MyType) — writes a single fragment with print, no trailing newline.
First two statements of Base.show must be: Base.summary(io, x); println(io)
Subsequent lines indented by two spaces for readability.
Never print collection contents — only counts, sizes, or ranges.

Never allocate inside show or summary: use length(), size(), isempty(), and first() on lazy iterators (e.g. values(dict)) rather than collect(), sort(), or any function that copies a collection. All data access must be O(1).
When improving an existing Base.show (Category B): read the full method first; change only the lines that violate the rubric; do not restructure passing logic.
Use sprint(show, MIME("text/plain"), x) and sprint(summary, x) in tests.

Place methods adjacent to the type definition in the same source file (matching the existing convention in `src/ParameterDistributions.jl`). Place Base.summary immediately before Base.show. Do not create a separate `src/show.jl`.
formatting.example:
## ── Category A example ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ## Scenario: MyEnvironment holds a large Dict of DataFrames and a date range.
## Before (default Julia show — prints entire Dict contents)
julia> env MyEnvironment(data=Dict{String, DataFrame}(...dozens of rows...))
## After: summary + show pair
function Base.summary(io::IO, x::MyEnvironment) print(io, "MyEnvironment (n_basins=", length(x.data), ")") end
function Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::MyEnvironment) Base.summary(io, x); println(io) println(io, " date_range: ", x.start_date, " to ", x.end_date) end
# julia> env # MyEnvironment (n_basins=847) # date_range: 2000-01-01 to 2020-12-31
## Corresponding unit tests
@testset "MyEnvironment show" begin env = MyEnvironment(...) out = sprint(show, MIME("text/plain"), env) @test occursin("MyEnvironment", out) @test count(==('\n'), out) <= 10 sm = sprint(summary, env) @test occursin("MyEnvironment", sm) @test !isempty(sm) end
## ── Category B example ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ## Scenario: existing show prints sorted keys of a large Dict (allocation-unsafe).
## Before (violates allocation-safety criterion)
function Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::MyStore) println(io, "MyStore") for k in sort(collect(keys(x.data))) # allocates! println(io, " ", k, " => ", x.data[k]) end end
## After (minimal fix — replace allocation with O(1) count; add summary)
function Base.summary(io::IO, x::MyStore) print(io, "MyStore (n=", length(x.data), ")") end
function Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::MyStore) Base.summary(io, x); println(io) println(io, " n_entries: ", length(x.data)) end
